request.question: Who besides Marceau founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program that was featured in a 2005 American documentary?

title: 100 Classrooms program
Take the Lead
Yvonne Marceau
One World Youth Project
Potisarn Pittayakorn School
Pierre Dulaine
Mad Hot Ballroom
Index of Afghanistan-related articles
Theodore C. Marceau
2010 Panasonic Gobel Awards
sentences: The 100 Classrooms program was a joint project by Deutsche Welle and Cap Anamur to build and restore classrooms in Afghanistan after the U.S. invasion of 2001.
Beginning June 13, 2003, the program helped to build nearly 300 classrooms and 32 schools.
Take the Lead is a 2006 drama dance film directed by Liz Friedlander, and starring Antonio Banderas as dance instructor Pierre Dulaine, the founder of Dancing Classrooms.
It also stars Alfre Woodard, John Ortiz, Rob Brown, Yaya DaCosta, Dante Basco, Elijah Kelley, and Jenna Dewan.
The film was released on April 7, 2006.
Although based in New York City, it was filmed in Toronto.
Stock footage of various locations in New York City were used.
Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.
She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.
In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.
Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.
Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.
Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.

Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.
Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.
One World Youth Project (OWYP) is a non-profit 501(c)(3) corporation founded in Massachusetts and currently based in Washington DC.
The goal of One World Youth Project is to enhance education towards a more discerning, empathetic and empowered generation of global citizens.

In 2009, One World Youth Project launched the One World Hub program, a service-learning program that trains university students to lead a global education curriculum in local middle/high schools, and to connect these secondary school classrooms with partner classrooms in other countries.
The One World Youth Project global education curriculum includes the following units: cultural exchange, understanding of global challenges, community mapping, and service-learning.
Potisarn Pittayakorn is a coeducational school for grades 7 to 12 in Thailand.
Founded by Nuan Thamutaro (นวล ธมมธโร) in 1956, Potisarn is built on Mr. and Mrs. Saiputhong's land in an area of 6 rai 440 square wah.
When the school started in 1956, there was no classroom or any buildings.
Students had to study in a temple.
Later, Nuan Thamutaro raised funds for a school building.
The result was a 2-floor wooden building that contained 12 classrooms.
This building was finished in 1958.
In 1982, Potisarn School got funded for a 3-floor concrete building that contained 18 classrooms.
In 1983, the pool and toilet were built.
Later in 1984, the elementary education department selected Potisarn School into its elementary improvement program.
In this program, Potisarn's students got free books, free uniforms and free shelter.
In 1986, Suankularb Wittayalai recruited Potisarn to be their sub-school.
That meant any Potisarn students who met the requirements would be eligible to get into Suankalarb Wittayalai without any further exam.
Now the school consists of 3 main buildings, 1 medical building and 1 football field.
Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.
He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.
He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film directed and co-produced by Marilyn Agrelo and written and co-produced by Amy Sewell, about a ballroom dance program in the New York City Department of Education, the New York City public school system for fifth graders.
Several styles of dance are shown in the film, such as tango, foxtrot, swing, rumba and merengue.
– 100 Classrooms program
Theodore Christopher Marceau (May 28, 1859 - June 22, 1922) was an American photographer.
He pioneered the creation of a national chain of photographic studios in the United States in the 1880s.
He founded the "Marceau Studios" in Manhattan, New York City, Philadelphia and Boston and was one of the most widely known photographers in the country.

The 13th Annual Panasonic Gobel Awards (or the 13th Annual Panasonic Awards) honoring the favorite in television programming/production works/individual, was held on March 26, 2010, at the Ballroom Theater Djakarta XXI in Jalan M.H. Thamrin, Menteng, Central Jakarta.
RCTI, Global TV and RCTI televised the ceremony in the Indonesia.
This 2010 ceremony awards this issue titled "Indonesia Unite".
The event coincides with the Golden Year of PT Panasonic Gobel Indonesia, as the organizer of Panasonic Awards.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who besides Marceau founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program that was featured in a 2005 American documentary?
hop1_docs: Yvonne Marceau | Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.  She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.  In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.  Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.  Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.  Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.  Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.  Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.

Mad Hot Ballroom | Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film directed and co-produced by Marilyn Agrelo and written and co-produced by Amy Sewell, about a ballroom dance program in the New York City Department of Education, the New York City public school system for fifth graders.  Several styles of dance are shown in the film, such as tango, foxtrot, swing, rumba and merengue.

Pierre Dulaine | Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.  He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.  He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Champion Ballroom Academy | The Champion Ballroom Academy (founded April 1990) is a dance studio in San Diego, California.  The studio's main specialties are social partner-dancing, competitive ballroom dance (aka.  Dancesport) and the Latin-dance-based aerobic program called Core Rhythms.

Warren Bullock | Warren Bullock is a professional ballroom dancer and dance teacher.  He owns a dance teaching business with a chain of 14 'studios', and also teaches on cruises.  He has won a number of awards as a dance teacher and coach and together with his wife Jane, pioneered the teaching of Ballroom and Latin American dancing in schools starting at Glebefields School in Tipton and King Edwards School for girls in Edgebaston before speaking and promoting this concept at the BDF conference in 1997 which resulted in dance becoming part of the national curriculum in some counties.  He was the main character in 8 one hour programmes of Baby Ballroom on channel 5Star - a reality documentary made by Firecracker Films based on Bullock, his family and Zig Zag Dance Factory <ref>http://www.channel5.com/show/baby-ballroom/ <ref>http://www.firecrackerfilms.com/broadcast/baby-ballroom/

Ballroom Bootcamp | Ballroom Bootcamp was an American reality television series that debuted on the TLC cable network, on October 7, 2005.  The series follows several individuals who try to improve their skill of dance.  The series ended on January 26, 2007, after two seasons.

Louis Van Amstel | Louis van Amstel (born June 23, 1972) is a Dutch-American ballroom dance champion, professional dancer, and choreographer who appears on the U.S. reality television series "Dancing with the Stars".  He is the creator of the popular dance fitness program LaBlast.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program / Mad Hot Ballroom (2005 documentary)

- From passage [1]: Yvonne Marceau "co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine" (1984) and "Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom."
- From passage [3]: "Pierre Dulaine ... founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children."
- No ambiguity in the passages: the only other named founder candidate is "Pierre Dulaine" (no alternative co-founders listed).
- Missing facts / bridge if more detail needed: exact founding date of the Dancing Classrooms program (not given); more on the program's institutional name variant ("Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms" vs. "Dancing Classrooms").

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. No additional retrieval needed to identify the co-founder (passages explicitly name Pierre Dulaine) — defer final answer production to downstream component.
2. (If verification desired) Fetch the "Pierre Dulaine" page — inspect lede/infobox and sections "Dancing Classrooms" or "Career."
3. Fetch the "Yvonne Marceau" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Career" for corroboration.
4. Fetch "Mad Hot Ballroom" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Synopsis" to confirm program naming and film linkage.
response.hop2_query: Yvonne Marceau Pierre Dulaine Dancing Classrooms cofounder evidence
hop2_docs: Yvonne Marceau | Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.  She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.  In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.  Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.  Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.  Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.  Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.  Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.

Pierre Dulaine | Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.  He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.  He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Take the Lead | Take the Lead is a 2006 drama dance film directed by Liz Friedlander, and starring Antonio Banderas as dance instructor Pierre Dulaine, the founder of Dancing Classrooms.  It also stars Alfre Woodard, John Ortiz, Rob Brown, Yaya DaCosta, Dante Basco, Elijah Kelley, and Jenna Dewan.  The film was released on April 7, 2006.  Although based in New York City, it was filmed in Toronto.  Stock footage of various locations in New York City were used.

Yvonne Jospa | Yvonne Jospa ("née" Have Groisman, February 3, 1910 in Poputi, Bessarabia – January 20, 2000 in Brussels) was a cofounder and leading organizer of the "Comité de Défense des Juifs" in September 1942 with her husband Hertz Jospa, which saved over 3,000 Jewish children from deportation and death.  Yvonne Jaspar was her pseudonym in the Belgian Resistance.
Église Saint-Pierre-de-Chaillot | The église Saint-Pierre-de-Chaillot is a Roman Catholic church in the 16th arrondissement of Paris, at 31, avenue Marceau, almost at its corner with rue de Chaillot.

Yvonne Chouteau | Myra Yvonne Chouteau (March 7, 1929 – January 24, 2016) was one of the "Five Moons" or Native "prima ballerinas" of Oklahoma.  She was the only child of Col. Corbett Edward and Lucy Arnett Chouteau.  She was born March 7, 1929 in Fort Worth, Texas.  In 1943, she became the youngest dancer ever accepted to the Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo, where she worked for fourteen years, In 1962, she and her husband, Miguel Terekhov, founded the first fully accredited university dance program in the United States, the School of Dance at the University of Oklahoma.  A member of the Shawnee Tribe, she is also of ethnic French ancestry, the great-great-great-granddaughter of Maj. Jean Pierre Chouteau.  From the Chouteau family of St. Louis, he established Oklahoma's oldest European-American settlement, at the present site of Salina, in 1796.  She grew up in Vinita, Oklahoma.
Yvonne Suddick | Yvonne Suddick is a British figure skater who competed in ice dance.
response.summary_2: Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom. Pierre Dulaine is a dance instructor who founded Dancing Classrooms and devised the Dulaine method of teaching dance. Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer and choreographer.
